title: Rustic X Console Table
summary: The farmhouse classic: a planked-top console with X ends and decorative bolt hardware. The X is scribed in place, not cut from a drawing — that's the technique worth learning.
description:
The Rustic X is probably the most-built console design of the farmhouse era, and the X ends are why. What the plan actually teaches is scribing: instead of trusting the cut list's angles blindly, you hold the uncut X board against its opening, trace the overlaps in pencil, and cut the lines. Wood by wood, your table's real dimensions — not the drawing's — define the joint. The listed 60°/30° cuts get you close; the pencil gets you tight.

The order matters for the X: fit the long diagonal first, leave it loose, fit and cut the two short pieces against it, then join the shorts to the long OUTSIDE the table and install the whole X as one unit. Trying to assemble it inside the opening is the classic frustration this sequence avoids.

Two checks the plan flags. The 2x4 end supports must match your actual 1x12 width — nominally 11-1/4", but measure, because shelf trim that overhangs or falls short at the ends is glaring. And the corner hardware with decorative bolts is what sells the industrial-farmhouse look; it's cosmetic over structural screws, so position it by eye, symmetrically, after finishing.

tools.note: Frames, shelf trim, and top — 3/4" and 1-1/2" settings both get used.
The 60°/30° X-piece angles exceed most miter saw gauges — cut them with the board on edge, or scribe and cut with a circular saw.
For cutting the scribed X lines — a jigsaw works too.
The X pieces are scribed in place — the pencil is a precision tool in this build.

materials.name: 1x12, 12 ft
materials.note: The two shelves. Measure its true width — the end supports are cut to match it.

materials.name: Corner hardware and decorative bolts
materials.note: The industrial-farmhouse jewelry. Cosmetic — position by eye after finishing.

cutList.note: Cut to match your 1x12's TRUE width — measure first.
Long point to short point, ends parallel, 60° off square — then scribe-fit in place.
Long end 60°, short end 30°, angles same direction — scribe-fit against the installed long diagonal.

steps.title: Build the two end frames
steps.body: Assemble each end frame from 2x2 uprights and 2x4 rails per the diagram — pocket holes on the faces that end up hidden. These frames are the table's whole skeleton; square them carefully.

steps.title: Join the frames with the shelves
steps.body: Before attaching, drill 3/4" pocket holes around all edges of both 1x12 shelves — you'll want them later for the shelf trim, and drilling them now beats drilling inside an assembled table. Then attach the frames to the shelves with 1-1/4" pocket screws.

steps.title: Fit and attach the end supports
steps.body: Measure your 1x12s' actual width and cut the five 2x4 end supports to exactly that — nominal 11-1/4", real boards vary. Attach to the frames and shelves, 1-1/2" pocket holes with 2-1/2" pocket screws into the frames.

steps.title: Trim the shelves
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 shelf trim using the pocket holes you pre-drilled in step 2, with 1-1/4" screws. The trim thickens the shelf edge visually to match the table's chunky frame.

steps.title: Scribe and build the X ends
steps.body:
Hold the long diagonal board against its opening and trace the overlaps at both ends. Cut the lines, set it in place — but DON'T attach. Fit the two short pieces against it the same way: place, trace, cut.

Now trace where the shorts meet the long piece, pull everything out, and join the shorts to the long diagonal outside the table. Install the completed X as one unit and fasten. Repeat for the other end.

Scribing beats measuring here because the X inherits your table's true geometry — including every sixteenth it differs from the drawing.

steps.title: Top it and add the hardware
steps.body:
Join the three 2x6 top planks with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket screws (or lay them individually for a more rustic gappy top — both are legitimate), then fasten the top down through the aprons using the same 2-1/2" pocket screws.

Sand, stain, topcoat. Last, the costume: hammer-set the corner hardware and decorative bolts symmetrically at the frame corners. It's fake structure over real screws, and it's what makes people ask where you bought the table.

images.alt: Farmhouse console table with X-brace ends, planked top, and decorative bolt hardware
